# Getting a Certificate

**Article**

Obtain a certificate from an identity, from DER-encoded data, or from the keychain.

## Overview

You use functions of the certificate, key, and trust services API to manage and manipulate certificates. The API defines the [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/SecCertificate] opaque type to hold certificate objects. You typically use such an object to indicate the certificate to use for a particular cryptographic operation, such as evaluating trust. How you get a certificate reference depends on where the certificate is stored or how it’s provided to you. For example, it might come from any of the following:

- **An identity.** When you read an identity from a password-protected file or from the keychain (as described in [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/importing-an-identity] and [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/storing-an-identity-in-the-keychain], respectively), you can extract the certificate it contains (see [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/parsing-an-identity]). In macOS, you can also store a certificate in an identity along with its associated private key (see [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/creating-an-identity]).
- **DER-encoded data.** You can export a certificate as DER-encoded data that you then store on disk or send through the network. Similarly, you can restore a certificate from this kind of data. See [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/storing-a-der-encoded-x-509-certificate] for details.
- **The keychain.** Just as with keys and passwords, you can store a certificate in a keychain and later retrieve it from there. See [doc://com.apple.security/documentation/Security/storing-a-certificate-in-the-keychain] for details.
title: Getting a Certificate
